lessons.title: Introduction to Charcoal
lessons.instructions: Read the basics of what charcoal is and why grillers choose it. Click Next to continue.
learning_objectives: Understand how charcoal is produced and why it's used in grilling
Learn how charcoal creates the distinctive smoky flavor
Recognize that different charcoal types have different properties

sections.heading: Definition
sections.body: Charcoal is a carbon-rich material produced by slowly heating wood without oxygen. In grilling it delivers intense, even heat — far hotter than a gas flame can sustain over a full cook.

sections.heading: Flavor
sections.body: As charcoal burns it releases aromatic compounds that bond to the surface of whatever you are cooking. That is where the unmistakable smoky flavor of charcoal-grilled food comes from.

sections.heading: Why it matters
sections.body: Different charcoal types burn at different temperatures and for different lengths of time. Picking the right one is the first real decision a new griller makes.

lessons.title: Types of Charcoal
lessons.instructions: Tap any charcoal type to see its stats. Your pick is recorded so we can reference it later.
learning_objectives: Identify four common types of charcoal used in grilling
Compare charcoal types by heat output, burn duration, and consistency
Choose the right charcoal type based on cooking needs

sections.heading: Four common options
sections.body: There is no single 'best' charcoal — the right choice depends on how long you need to cook and how hot you need to go.

interaction.prompt: Which one fits the cook you have in mind?

options.label: Briquettes
options.summary: Uniform pillow-shaped lumps. Consistent, affordable, and easy to light. A great default.

options.label: Lump
options.summary: Chunks of natural hardwood. Lights fast and burns very hot, but it burns out quickly.

options.label: Competition Briquettes
options.summary: Premium briquettes used by pitmasters. Long, steady burn at a high, consistent heat.

options.label: Bamboo
options.summary: Eco-friendly and sustainable. Produces a distinct aroma and a moderately long burn.

lessons.title: Grilling Techniques — The Big Picture
lessons.instructions: Skim the four techniques. The next page lets you compare them side by side.
learning_objectives: Name four fundamental charcoal grilling techniques
Understand the heat characteristics of each technique
Identify which technique suits different cooking styles

sections.heading: Two-Zone
sections.body: Pile coals on one side of the grill and leave the other side empty. You get a hot zone for searing and a cool zone for finishing — the most forgiving setup for beginners.

sections.heading: Snake Method
sections.body: Arrange unlit coals in a C-shape around the edge, light one end, and let the burn creep slowly around. Produces hours of low, steady heat — ideal for ribs and brisket.

sections.heading: Direct Heat
sections.body: Coals spread evenly under the entire grate for maximum heat everywhere. Best for fast cooks like burgers and hot dogs.

sections.heading: Charcoal + Wood Mix
sections.body: Drop chunks of hardwood on top of lit coals for an extra layer of smoke flavor. Great for smoked wings and pork shoulder.

lessons.title: Charcoal Layouts in Practice
lessons.instructions: Imagine the dish you want to cook, then tap the layout you would use.
learning_objectives: Apply grilling techniques to real cooking scenarios
Match cooking methods to desired food outcomes
Plan layout selection based on cook duration and heat needs

sections.heading: Match the food to the layout
sections.body: Each layout plays to different strengths. Your selection helps us recommend a first cook at the end.

interaction.prompt: Which layout are you most curious to try?

options.label: Two-Zone
options.best_for: Chicken thighs, bone-in cuts, thick steaks
options.summary: Sear over the hot side, then slide to the cool side to finish without burning.

options.label: Snake Method
options.summary: Low, steady heat for hours. Set it up, close the lid, and let time do the work.

options.label: Direct Heat
options.summary: Maximum heat everywhere. Fast cooks with a strong sear.

options.label: Charcoal + Wood Mix
options.summary: Adds wood-smoke flavor on top of a charcoal base. Great when flavor matters more than speed.

lessons.title: Safety & Disposal
lessons.instructions: The last lesson. Read the safety checklist, then head to the quiz.
learning_objectives: Understand proper charcoal cooling and extinguishing procedures
Learn safe disposal methods for used charcoal
Recognize the hazards of improperly cooled charcoal

sections.heading: 1. Make sure the coals are cold
sections.body: After cooking, close all vents and let the coals burn down. They can stay hot for up to 24 hours — never assume they are out just because the flames are gone.

sections.heading: 2. Extinguish with water, not just air
sections.body: Carefully pour water on the coals and stir until no steam rises and no glowing embers remain. This is the only reliable way to confirm the fire is fully out.

sections.heading: 3. Dispose in a metal container
sections.body: Used charcoal is not recyclable and not compostable. Once the ashes are completely cool, seal them in a metal container and place them in the regular trash.

quiz.question: Which charcoal lasts the longest?
explanations: Briquettes burn for 3–4 hours, which is long, but not the longest.
Lump charcoal burns out quickly in 1–1.5 hours.
Correct! Competition Briquettes are premium charcoal designed for long, steady burns of 4–6 hours.
Bamboo burns for 2–3 hours, moderate duration.
quiz.key_takeaway: Competition Briquettes are engineered for marathon cooks.

quiz.question: Which charcoal burns the hottest?
explanations: Briquettes peak at 650–700°F.
Correct! Lump charcoal reaches 800–1000°F, the hottest of all types.
Competition Briquettes peak at 700–800°F, high but not the hottest.
Bamboo reaches 600–700°F.
quiz.key_takeaway: Lump charcoal is ideal for high-heat searing.

quiz.question: Which charcoal is the most consistent?
explanations: Briquettes are 95% consistent, very reliable.
Lump is only 60–75% consistent because it's chunks of natural wood.
Correct! Competition Briquettes are 95–98% consistent, the most reliable for precise temperature control.
Bamboo is 80–90% consistent.
quiz.key_takeaway: Competition Briquettes offer the best predictability.

quiz.question: You are cooking ribs for several hours and need steady, low heat. Which charcoal layout should you use?
explanations: Direct Heat spreads coals everywhere for maximum heat—too hot for a long, slow cook.
Two-Zone is great for searing and finishing, but doesn't provide the steady, low heat ribs need.
Correct! The Snake Method creates a slow burn that creeps around the grill, perfect for hours of low, steady heat.
Charcoal + Wood Mix adds flavor but isn't the best for sustained low heat over many hours.
quiz.key_takeaway: The Snake Method is the go-to for low-and-slow cooking like ribs and brisket.

quiz.question: In the U.S., which waste category should used charcoal be disposed in?
explanations: Charcoal is not recyclable.
Charcoal is not compostable.
Correct! Used charcoal goes in the general trash once it is completely cool.
Charcoal only goes in the trash—not the other categories.
quiz.key_takeaway: Cool, used charcoal is regular waste, not a special material.
